behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real filesystem watching behavior, not pure local logic. The behavior text requires recursive watch semantics across real directories ("inside a subdirectory of the watched root"). A mock watcher can trivially emit a subdirectory event and hide bugs in OS watcher configuration/recursive subscription.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temp input/output directory tree, create a nested subdirectory under the watched root, write a file into that subdirectory, and assert exactly one transformed output appears. This requires real OS filesystem events.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is about local dedup decision logic keyed by path+time window. It can be reproduced with one object/function and controlled time inputs; no OS/network/subprocess is required. Higher levels are disqualified by the behavior text focusing on "deduplication logic" and "same file path within a configurable time window," which can be exercised deterministically without watcher I/O.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the dedup component with a known window, submit request A for path P, then submit a second request for P inside the window and assert it is discarded; advance fake clock beyond the window and assert acceptance. No filesystem watcher needed.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly depends on OS event duplication ("operating system emits multiple filesystem events"). Integration fakes can inject duplicates but may miss real watcher burst/coalescing patterns that cause double-processing in production. By tie-breaker evidence, lower level fails because the concrete failure is tied to real OS emission behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with a real watched directory, perform a file write pattern known to trigger multiple events (e.g., write+rename or chunked write), capture processing invocations/output, and assert one processing result for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This is OS-level directory lifecycle behavior (delete/recreate, inode changes, watcher reattachment). In-process mocks typically do not reproduce real watcher invalidation semantics after directory replacement. The behavior text ("removed and then recreated while the daemon is running") directly requires real filesystem primitives.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon watching a real directory, delete the watched directory, recreate it with the same path, then create a new file and assert it is processed without restarting the daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering depends on concurrent arrival timing plus real file metadata acquisition. A pure in-process fake can miss failures caused by real timestamp resolution/platform semantics and watcher event interleaving under simultaneous writes. Higher adjacent level is justified by behavior text "arrive at the same time" and "creation timestamps," which ties correctness to real filesystem timing/metadata.
behaviors.test_description: With daemon running on a real directory, create several files nearly simultaneously, then verify observed processing sequence matches ascending creation timestamp as read by the daemon (including deterministic tie-handling if needed).

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Failure appears at real OS write/event timing boundary: arrival event may fire before file is stable. Mocked writes/events usually hide partial-write races and file-lock/flush timing behaviors. The behavior text explicitly requires this race condition ("still being written to when the arrival event fires").
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real watched directory, open a file and write it in chunks with delays while keeping it incomplete, confirm processing does not start mid-write, then close/finish write and assert processing starts afterward once complete.
